does: This paper identifies and characterizes Document-Authored Control-Signal Impersonation (DACSI), a vulnerability in retrieval-augmented generation systems where attacker-controlled document text can masquerade as legitimate control signals like metadata, provenance markers, or policy labels. The authors demonstrate that when RAG systems serialize diverse information sources into a single natural-language prompt, they create an opportunity for injected documents to impersonate authorization or disclosure-policy signals that models treat as binding. The paper evaluates DACSI across six model configurations with varying prompt pressures and injection baselines, showing that this non-imperative attack pattern exploits the ambiguity between trusted control signals and untrusted document content rendered in the same channel, with systematic evaluation of source-authority attribution probes and RAG-mediated pipeline vulnerabilities.
connects: This work extends the prompt injection vulnerability literature by identifying a distinct subclass of indirect attacks that differs from command-style injection attacks studied in prior work. Rather than asking models to override policy through explicit imperatives, DACSI exploits a fundamental design flaw in how RAG systems collapse multiple information sources into unified prompts, a problem recognized in recent RAG safety research. The paper builds on observations about natural-language channel mixing and draws from prior work on metadata manipulation and authority boundary failures in multi-source systems. It connects to broader research on prompt injection, instruction hierarchy understanding, and the challenge of maintaining semantic separation in concatenated text, while positioning DACSI as a critical vulnerability class that current RAG designs inherit from their prompt rendering architecture.
enables: This work establishes DACSI as a distinct attack vector that could drive future RAG system redesigns toward explicit signal separation rather than relying on models to distinguish control signals from document content through language understanding alone. It points toward architectural solutions like token-level or structural separation of trusted metadata from retrieved documents, formal models of source authority, and robustness techniques that maintain clear boundaries between different information classes. The findings motivate research into RAG prompt languages that syntactically distinguish control signals from content, detection methods for control-signal impersonation, and evaluation frameworks for RAG safety that specifically probe source-authority boundary integrity. The work also opens questions about whether models can reliably infer source attribution and whether alternative serialization formats could fundamentally mitigate this class of vulnerability.
